content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

February 9, 2016

Vera M.I. Todorov
Senior Deputy City Attorney
200 East Santa Clara Street, 16th Floor Tower
San Jose, CA 95113-1905

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-16-020

Dear Ms. Todorov:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of San Jose Planning Commissioner
Shiloh Ballard regarding the Commissioner's duties under the conflict of interest provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of
interest or Section 1090.

QUESTION

Does the Act prevent Commissioner Ballard from participating in decisions on applications
for entitlements before the Planning Commission brought by donors to the Commissioner's
nonprofit employer, the Silicon Valley Bicycle Coalition ("SVBC")?

CONCLUSION

Since the governmental decisions in question will not have a financial effect on the
Commissioner or the Commissioner's employer, the Commissioner will not have a conflict of
interest under the Act.

FACTS

Commissioner Ballard has been a member of the San Jose Planning Commission since her
appointment on February 24, 2015. The Commissioner's term ends on June 30, 2016, but she can
be reappointed by the City Council.

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-16-020
Page No. 2

Commissioner Ballard is, in her private capacity, the Chief Executive Officer of an IRS 501
(c)(3) non-profit called SVBC that is formed to promote a better community, environment and
economy through increased bicycling. SVBC has three main revenue streams: grants/contracts,
individual donations, and events. The total Fiscal Year 2015-2016 budget for the non-profit is
$950,000, with approximately $200,000 of that annual budget coming from donations of goods,
services, and funds. For example, SVBC received a donation of $500 in beer from Gordon Biersch,
a local brewery, for event and after-parties. Apple, Inc. donated $10,000 as a co-sponsor of Bike-to-
Work Month in May 2015. The largest corporate donation per year is $25,000.

Late last year, the Planning Commission considered a development permit application that
affected a site leased by the Gordon Biersch Brewery, and a few weeks ago the Planning
Commission considered a recommendation to the City Council to approve a development
agreement and other related entitlements for the Apple, Inc. campus in North San Jose.
Commissioner Ballard has abstained from participating in any decision regarding the above-
specified matters.

ANALYSIS

Conflicts of Interest under the Act

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official has a financial interest. The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions only apply to conflicts of
interests based on the following:

• Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment worth
$2,000 or more. (Section 87103(a).)

• Any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth $2,000
or more. (Section 87103(b).)

• Any source of income, except gifts or loans by a commercial lending institution made in the
regular course of business on terms available to the public without regard to official status,
aggregating $500 or more in value provided or promised to, received by, the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made. (Section 87103(c).)

• Any business entity in which the public official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d).)

• Any donor of, or any intermediary or agent for a donor of, a gift or gifts aggregating $460 or
more in value provided to, received by, or promised to the public official within 12 months
prior to the time when the decision is made. (Section 87103(e).)

• In addition, a public official's personal finances are deemed to be directly involved in a
governmental decision that will have any financial effect on his or her personal finances or
those of his or her immediate family. (Section 87103.)

File No. A-16-020
Page No. 3

You stated that Commissioner Ballard is employed by SVBC, a 501(c)(3) charitable
nonprofit. While a nonprofit is not a "business entity" for purposes of Section 87103(a) and (d) (see
Section 82005), it will still be considered a source of income to the Commissioner because she
receives $500 or more annually from SVBC. (Section 87103(c).)

Foreseeability and Materiality: Having concluded that SVBC is a potentially disqualifying
source of income to the Commissioner, the next step in the conflict of interest analysis is to
determine if the specific governmental decisions will have a foreseeable and material financial
effect on the source of income.

The decisions you describe are entitlement decisions initiated by donors to SVBC;

Generally, a financial effect is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the interest is
"explicitly involved" in a decision. An interest is "explicitly involved" in a decision if the interest is
a named party in, or the subject of, a governmental decision before the official or the official's
agency. (Regulation 18701(a).) Under your facts SVBC will not be "explicitly involved" in the
decisions in question. When the interest is "not explicitly involved" in the decision, a financial
effect is reasonably foreseeable if the effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more
than hypothetical or theoretical. Neither of these governmental decisions will have any possible
effect on SVBC. Thus, we conclude that the decisions do not result in a foreseeable, material
financial effect on SVBC.

Nexus: In addition to the materiality standards for a source of income as described above,
there is a special rule for materiality that applies in cases where there is a "nexus" between duties
owed to a source of income and to the official's public agency. When a public official is paid by
another entity to accomplish some action that is within the official's public decision-making
authority, any financial effect on the source of income is presumed material, regardless of whether
the source of income is directly involved or indirectly involved. The "nexus test" is set out at
Regulation 18705.3(c):

"Nexus. Any reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a person who is a source
of income to a public official is deemed material if the public official receives or
is promised the income to achieve a goal or purpose which would be achieved,
defeated, aided, or hindered by the decision."

The rationale for the nexus test is that when an employee earns a salary to accomplish a purpose
that may be advanced by what he or she does as a public official, we presume that the private
employer is benefiting from the actions of the employee in his or her official capacity. (Yarnell
Advice Letter, No. A-00-161.) Typically, a "nexus" is found in situations where the official is also a
high-level employee with direct influence and control over his or her employer's management or
policy decisions. (Moser Advice Letter, No. A-03-147; Low Advice Letter, No. A-99-305.)
However, nexus has always been premised on the fact that there will be a financial effect on the
source. (See e.g., Hopper Advice Letter, No. I-11-100.) Because the two types of governmental
decisions described above will have no financial effect on SVBC, the nexus standard would not
apply.

File No. A-16-020
Page No. 4

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Assistant General Counsel
Legal Division

JWW:jgl

File No. A-16-02O

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does the Act prevent Commissioner Ballard from participating in decisions on applications 
for entitlements before the Planning Commission brought by donors to the Commissioner's 
nonprofit employer, the Silicon Valley Bicycle Coalition ("SVBC")?

CONCLUSION: Since the governmental decisions in question will not have a financial effect on the 
Commissioner or the Commissioner's employer, the Commissioner will not have a conflict of 
interest under the Act.
